content.text: Peer review of code changes before they are merged is a cornerstone of software quality, but in agentic AI systems it carries an extra dimension of responsibility. An agent that passes review gains the ability to execute real-world actions — sending emails, writing to databases, invoking external APIs — so a missed security flaw or a misread prompt change can produce side effects at scale and with speed that no human operator can easily reverse.
What it is

A code review is a structured process in which one or more engineers examine a proposed code change — typically surfaced as a pull request (PR) or merge request (MR) — before it is merged into the main branch. The reviewer reads the diff, asks questions, suggests improvements, and either requests changes or grants approval. The merge gate enforces that the required number of approvals is obtained and that automated checks (CI) pass before the change lands.

In an agentic AI repository the diff is not limited to application code. A single PR may touch Python logic, system-prompt files, tool schema definitions, evaluation datasets, configuration manifests, and infrastructure-as-code. Each of these carries its own risk surface, and each requires a reviewer who understands what that surface means for the agent's behavior at runtime. The review process therefore needs to be structured across multiple disciplines — ML, platform engineering, and security — rather than treated as a single pass by one generalist.

Code review is distinct from automated linting, static analysis, and CI evaluations, though it complements them. Automated tools catch syntax errors, style violations, and deterministic rule violations. Human review catches semantic errors: a tool that leaks data to an untrusted recipient, a prompt change that subtly widens the agent's autonomy, or an orchestration loop that can dead-lock under low-probability conditions. Neither replaces the other.
Why it matters

Agents execute side effects. Unlike a library function that computes a value, an agent tool call can delete a file, charge a credit card, or publish a message to thousands of subscribers. The asymmetry between the cost of reviewing a change and the cost of shipping a bad one is enormous. A single unreviewed destructive tool permission can produce irreversible harm before an on-call engineer can respond.

The agentic context also raises the stakes for prompt changes. A one-line edit to a system prompt might suppress a confirmation step, change the agent's persona, or remove a safety instruction. None of these changes produce a syntax error; none of them are caught by a linter. Only a human reviewer reading the prompt in context can assess whether the behavioral change is intentional and safe.

Beyond catching bugs, review is the primary mechanism for spreading architectural knowledge across a team. When a senior engineer reviews an orchestration loop and explains why the retry logic needs a jitter parameter, every future author who reads that review thread learns the pattern. This accumulative effect is especially valuable in agentic AI, where the design space is new and best practices are still being established team by team.
Benefits
Risk containment
— A second pair of eyes catches dangerous tool permissions, missing authentication, and prompt injection surfaces before they reach production.
Knowledge diffusion
— Reviewers learn orchestration patterns, eval methodology, and tool design conventions by reading PRs, reducing bus-factor risk.
Quality enforcement
— The review gate is the practical checkpoint where tests, documentation, and eval results are verified before code ships.
Accountability trail
— An approved PR carries a signed record of who read the change and judged it acceptable, which is essential for regulated industries and post-incident analysis.
Design pressure
— Authors simplify their changes when they know a reviewer will question anything hard to understand. Complexity that survives only because it was never examined rarely survives a review.
Onboarding acceleration
— New team members learn the codebase by reviewing small PRs before being trusted to author them, with low risk of production impact.
Trade-offs
Latency vs. throughput
— Every PR that waits for a reviewer is a change that is not yet deployed. On fast-moving teams, review queues become the primary bottleneck for shipping new agent capabilities.
Inconsistent depth

— Without explicit review norms, reviewers differ dramatically in what they examine. One reviewer blocks on missing tests; another rubber-stamps. The process is only as strong as its weakest approver.
Large-diff fatigue
— A PR that modifies prompt files, tool schemas, and application code simultaneously overwhelms any reviewer. Coverage drops sharply above ~400 lines of diff.
Expertise mismatch

— A general software engineer may not be qualified to review a prompt change for safety implications, or a tool implementation for authentication correctness. Single-discipline reviews miss cross-disciplinary risks.
Review theater
— Under deadline pressure, reviewers skim and approve. This creates a false sense of safety while providing none of the actual benefits of review.
How it works

The standard review cycle begins when an author opens a pull request against the main branch. The PR description should contain: the motivation for the change, a link to any relevant issue or design doc, the specific files changed and why, eval results or experiment IDs if the change touches prompts or agent logic, and a callout of any security-sensitive areas that deserve closer scrutiny.
Automated checks run first

CI triggers immediately on PR open. For agent repos this typically includes: linting and type-checking, unit tests, integration tests against a sandboxed environment, a secret-scanning pass, and one or more automated eval suites that measure agent behavior on a golden dataset. The review gate should require all of these to pass before a human reviewer's approval counts — approving a PR with failing evals defeats the purpose of having evals.
Human review follows CI

Once CI is green, human reviewers examine the diff. In a well-structured agent team, CODEOWNERS assigns specific reviewers automatically by file path: the ML team owns prompt files, the platform team owns tool implementations, and the security team is required on any PR that adds a new external API integration. Reviewers leave inline comments on specific lines, and the author responds — either making the requested change, asking a clarifying question, or explaining why the existing approach is correct.
Review categories and their weight

A useful mental taxonomy for review comments distinguishes blocking issues from non-blocking suggestions: a 'blocker' must be resolved before approval; a 'nit' is optional. Within blockers, risk-ranked categories guide where to spend review time: (1) security and safety — missing auth, excessive permissions, prompt injection surface; (2) correctness — logic bugs, off-by-one, race conditions; (3) test coverage — untested code paths for deterministic logic; (4) design — approach is unnecessarily complex or fragile; (5) style — automate this with a linter instead of spending human attention on it.
Merge and post-merge

After all required approvals and CI checks pass, the author (or an automated system) merges the PR, typically with a squash-merge to keep the main branch history linear. The merge commit SHA becomes the deployment unit. For agent capabilities that are gradually rolled out, feature flags tied to the SHA allow the rollout to be paused or reverted without a full revert commit.
Architecture

The diagram below shows the full review flow for an agentic AI repository, from the author pushing a branch through CI checks, conditional security review, peer review, and the three outcome paths: approved, changes requested (loop back to author), or blocked by security. CODEOWNERS configuration drives automatic reviewer assignment based on which directories the diff touches.

attrs.alt: Code review flow for an agentic AI system — author pushes, CI runs, security review for new tools, peer review, then approve/iterate/block outcomes

content.text: The critical design insight is that security review is a conditional branch, not a sequential step applied to every PR. Most PRs — bug fixes, refactors, documentation — do not touch the external tool surface and can be approved by peer reviewers alone. Security review is automatically required (via CODEOWNERS) only when the diff touches tool definitions, network-calling code, or permission manifests. This keeps the high-friction security review path proportional to actual risk while maintaining a fast lane for lower-risk changes.
When to use it
Always require review
All merges to the main branch of a team agent repository, regardless of author seniority.
Any change to a system prompt, tool schema, or agent configuration file.
Any change that adds a new external API call, network endpoint, or file-system write path.
Dependency version bumps that include security fixes or breaking changes.
Require two reviews
Changes that grant an agent a destructive capability: delete, overwrite, publish, send.
Changes to production prompt files with no eval regression data attached.
Any change that removes or weakens a safety constraint or confirmation step.
Self-review is acceptable
Solo projects and prototypes where no second engineer exists — but still open a PR to get CI history and a reviewable record of decisions.
Documentation-only changes with no code or config impact.
When not to use blocking review
Hotfix paths for production incidents — use an expedited two-person verbal review and create a follow-up PR for full review post-incident.
Auto-generated asset files (lock files, compiled bundles) — verify via diff tooling rather than line-by-line human review.
How to implement

Setting up a robust review process for an agent repo requires configuring both the repository host (GitHub, GitLab) and team norms. The following walkthrough covers GitHub, but the patterns apply to any platform.
Step 1 — Branch protection rules

In GitHub repository Settings > Branches > Branch protection rules, require: at least one approval before merging, dismiss stale approvals when new commits are pushed, require status checks to pass (list each CI job by name), require branches to be up to date before merging, and block force-pushes to main.
Step 2 — CODEOWNERS

CODEOWNERS maps file paths to required reviewer teams. When a PR diff includes any file matching a pattern, the mapped team is automatically added as a required reviewer. This replaces ad-hoc reviewer assignment and ensures domain experts are always in the loop.

# .github/CODEOWNERS
# Syntax: <path-pattern>  <@team-or-@user> ...

# Prompt files — ML team must review all prompt changes
/prompts/                    @org/ml-team

# Tool implementations — platform and security teams required
/tools/                      @org/platform-team @org/security-team
/src/tools/                  @org/platform-team @org/security-team

# Agent orchestration core
/src/orchestrator/           @org/agent-core

# Evaluation datasets and metrics — ML team only
/evals/                      @org/ml-team

# Infrastructure / secrets management
/infra/                      @org/devops-team @org/security-team

# Catch-all: any file not matched above requires agent-core review
*                            @org/agent-core
Step 3 — PR description template
A PR template in .github/pull_request_template.md nudges authors to include all information reviewers need, reducing back-and-forth.

## What changed
<!-- Short description of the change and the motivation behind it. -->

## Type of change
- [ ] Bug fix
- [ ] New agent capability / tool
- [ ] Prompt change
- [ ] Refactor (no behavior change)
- [ ] Configuration / infrastructure

## Eval results
<!-- Required for prompt and tool changes. Link eval run or paste summary table. -->
<!-- If not applicable, explain why. -->

## Security considerations
<!-- Does this change add new network calls, file writes, or external permissions? -->
<!-- If yes: describe the authentication mechanism, scope limits, and any allowlists. -->

## Test coverage
- [ ] Unit tests added or updated
- [ ] Integration tests cover the new path
- [ ] Eval suite passes with no regression

## Checklist
- [ ] Self-reviewed the diff
- [ ] No secrets or credentials in the diff
- [ ] PR is scoped to a single logical change
Examples
Example 1 — Automated pre-review checklist (Python)

The following script runs locally as a pre-push hook or in CI. It inspects the staged diff for common agent-specific anti-patterns and prints a structured report before the PR is even opened. This surfaces obvious issues before a human reviewer ever sees the PR.

#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""
pre_review_check.py — runs on every push to catch common agentic AI anti-patterns.
Usage: python3 pre_review_check.py --base main
"""

import subprocess
import re
import sys
import argparse

BLOCKERS = [
    # Pattern, explanation
    (r"api_key\s*=\s*['"][^'"]{8,}", "Hardcoded API key detected"),
    (r"password\s*=\s*['"][^'"]{4,}", "Hardcoded password detected"),
    (r"allow_all\s*=\s*True", "allow_all=True disables permission checks"),
    (r"skip_confirmation\s*=\s*True", "Skipping confirmation removes human oversight"),
    (r"except\s*Exception\s*:\s*pass", "Silent exception swallowing hides agent errors"),
]

WARNINGS = [
    (r"TODO.*security", "Security TODO left in code"),
    (r"print\(.*token", "Token value may appear in logs"),
    (r"timeout\s*=\s*None", "Unbounded timeout can stall agent loops"),
]


def get_diff(base_branch: str) -> str:
    result = subprocess.run(
        ["git", "diff", f"{base_branch}...HEAD", "--unified=0"],
        capture_output=True, text=True, check=True
    )
    return result.stdout


def check_diff(diff: str):
    issues = []
    for i, line in enumerate(diff.splitlines(), 1):
        if not line.startswith("+") or line.startswith("+++"):
            continue
        code_line = line[1:]  # strip leading +
        for pattern, message in BLOCKERS:
            if re.search(pattern, code_line, re.IGNORECASE):
                issues.append(("BLOCKER", i, message, code_line.strip()))
        for pattern, message in WARNINGS:
            if re.search(pattern, code_line, re.IGNORECASE):
                issues.append(("WARNING", i, message, code_line.strip()))
    return issues


def main():
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser()
    parser.add_argument("--base", default="main")
    args = parser.parse_args()

    diff = get_diff(args.base)
    issues = check_diff(diff)

    if not issues:
        print("Pre-review check passed — no issues found.")
        sys.exit(0)

    blockers = [i for i in issues if i[0] == "BLOCKER"]
    warnings = [i for i in issues if i[0] == "WARNING"]

    for severity, lineno, message, snippet in issues:
        tag = "[BLOCKER]" if severity == "BLOCKER" else "[WARNING]"
        print(f"{tag} line ~{lineno}: {message}")
        print(f"         {snippet[:100]}")

    if blockers:
        print(f"\n{len(blockers)} blocker(s) found. Fix before opening PR.")
        sys.exit(1)
    else:
        print(f"\n{len(warnings)} warning(s). Review before merging.")
        sys.exit(0)


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
Example 2 — GitHub Actions workflow for agent eval gate (YAML / Python)

This CI workflow runs the agent evaluation suite on every PR. If the pass rate on the golden dataset drops below the required threshold, the check fails and the PR cannot be merged. The eval runner is a small Python script that calls the agent and scores responses.

# .github/workflows/eval-gate.yml
name: Agent eval gate

on:
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]
    paths:
      - "prompts/**"
      - "src/tools/**"
      - "src/orchestrator/**"

jobs:
  eval:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Set up Python
        uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with:
          python-version: "3.12"

      - name: Install dependencies
        run: pip install -r requirements.txt

      - name: Run eval suite
        env:
          ANTHROPIC_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.ANTHROPIC_API_KEY }}
        run: |
          python3 evals/run_eval.py \
            --golden-set evals/golden_set.jsonl \
            --min-pass-rate 0.92 \
            --output eval_results.json

      - name: Upload eval results
        uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        with:
          name: eval-results
          path: eval_results.json

# evals/run_eval.py — minimal eval runner called by the CI workflow above
import json
import argparse
import sys
from pathlib import Path
from typing import Any

# In a real repo, this would import your agent's run() function.
# Here we stub it to show the evaluation structure.

def run_agent(prompt: str, context: dict[str, Any]) -> str:
    """Call the agent with a prompt and return its response text."""
    # Replace with: from src.agent import run; return run(prompt, context)
    raise NotImplementedError("Wire up your agent here")


def score_response(response: str, expected: dict[str, Any]) -> bool:
    """Return True if the response meets the golden-set criteria."""
    if "contains" in expected:
        return all(kw.lower() in response.lower() for kw in expected["contains"])
    if "exact" in expected:
        return response.strip() == expected["exact"].strip()
    return True  # no scoring criteria means pass


def main():
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser()
    parser.add_argument("--golden-set", required=True)
    parser.add_argument("--min-pass-rate", type=float, default=0.90)
    parser.add_argument("--output", default="eval_results.json")
    args = parser.parse_args()

    cases = [json.loads(line) for line in Path(args.golden_set).read_text().splitlines() if line.strip()]
    results = []
    passed = 0

    for case in cases:
        try:
            response = run_agent(case["prompt"], case.get("context", {}))
            ok = score_response(response, case.get("expected", {}))
        except Exception as exc:
            response = f"ERROR: {exc}"
            ok = False

        results.append({"id": case.get("id"), "passed": ok, "response": response})
        if ok:
            passed += 1

    pass_rate = passed / len(cases) if cases else 0.0
    summary = {"pass_rate": pass_rate, "passed": passed, "total": len(cases), "results": results}

    Path(args.output).write_text(json.dumps(summary, indent=2))
    print(f"Pass rate: {pass_rate:.1%} ({passed}/{len(cases)})")

    if pass_rate < args.min_pass_rate:
        print(f"FAIL: below required {args.min_pass_rate:.1%}")
        sys.exit(1)

    print("PASS")
    sys.exit(0)


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
Real-world example

A product team building a customer-support agent wants to add a send_email tool that allows the agent to dispatch follow-up messages to customers. The PR includes: the tool implementation (Python), the updated tool schema (JSON), a change to the system prompt adding instructions for when to use the tool, and a set of eval cases covering both correct sends and scenarios where the agent should decline to send.

Because the diff touches /tools/ and /prompts/, CODEOWNERS automatically adds the platform team, the security team, and the ML team as required reviewers. The PR description includes a link to the eval run showing pass rate of 94.2% on a golden set of 240 cases, with a breakdown by failure mode.

The security reviewer checks four things: (1) SMTP credentials are read from the vault, not hardcoded; (2) the recipient address is validated against an allowlist of customer domains; (3) the tool requires a confirmation parameter set by the orchestrator before it sends; (4) the email content is sanitised to strip any HTML that could arrive via prompt injection from a customer message. The security reviewer requests one change: the allowlist check must also apply to CC and BCC addresses, which were not originally validated.

The ML reviewer checks the prompt instructions: the wording 'send an email when the customer asks' is too broad and would cause the agent to send emails for ambiguous requests. The reviewer requests a more conservative instruction with explicit examples of situations that do and do not warrant a send. The author updates the prompt, reruns the eval suite (pass rate improves to 96.1%), and posts the new results in the PR thread. Both reviewers approve, and the PR is squash-merged.
Common mistakes
Rubber-stamping without reading prompts

The most consequential changes in an agent repo are often prompt changes. A reviewer who approves a PR after reading only the Python diff and skipping the prompts/ directory may miss a change that silently widens the agent's autonomy or removes a safety guard. Treat prompt diffs with the same scrutiny as production code.
Reviewing style instead of risk

Review bandwidth is finite. Spending it on variable naming and formatting — both of which should be enforced by automated tools — leaves less attention for authentication logic and permission scope. Configure a linter, enforce it in CI, and commit to never blocking a PR on style if the linter passes.
Approving without CI green

Approving a PR and asking the author to 'fix the failing test later' decouples the approval from the quality signal that makes the approval meaningful. Require CI to pass before any approval is counted, and configure the merge gate to dismiss stale approvals when new commits are pushed.
Mega-PRs

A PR that modifies 2000 lines across prompts, tools, orchestration logic, and infrastructure is effectively unreviewed — no reviewer can hold that much context. Research consistently shows review effectiveness drops sharply above 400 lines. Authors should split large changes into a stack of small focused PRs, even if that requires coordinating merge order.

# Anti-pattern: one PR changes everything
# prompt update + new tool + infra change + eval dataset expansion
# Reviewer sees 1800-line diff → rubber-stamps

# Better pattern: split into three PRs with explicit dependencies
# PR 1: Add eval cases for the new behavior (no code changes, easy to review)
# PR 2: Implement the new tool with tests (reviewed by platform + security)
# PR 3: Update the prompt to use the tool, link PR-2 and eval results
Silent exception handling hiding agent errors
Best practices
Prioritise by risk, not by line count.
Spend the first ten minutes of any review on the highest-risk files: tool implementations, prompt changes, permission configuration. Read everything else after.
Ask questions rather than issue edicts.
'What happens if the recipient is in a different domain?' teaches more than 'Fix the allowlist.' It also gives the author room to explain context you may be missing.
Separate blocking comments from suggestions.
Use explicit labels ('blocker:' vs 'nit:') so the author knows what must be addressed before approval and what is optional feedback.
Require eval evidence for behavioral changes.
Any change to a prompt, tool schema, or agent configuration should be accompanied by eval results showing the behavioral delta is intentional and within acceptable bounds.
Time-box large PRs.
If a PR is too large to review thoroughly in 45 minutes, it is too large to merge. Request a split rather than approving superficially.
Keep review threads open until resolved.
Mark a thread resolved only when the author has made the requested change or the parties have agreed it is not needed — not just when the author replies.
Track review SLA.
Define a target response time (e.g., first review within one business day) and measure it. Long review queues signal a team capacity problem, not a PR problem.
Performance & scalability

As a team grows, the review process scales poorly by default: each PR requires reviewer attention, and reviewer attention is fixed. The naive approach — require everyone to review everything — produces a review bottleneck that scales as O(N) in the number of PRs and O(1/M) in the number of active reviewers (where M includes vacation, meetings, and other context-switching).

Effective scaling strategies fall into two categories: reducing review surface and distributing reviewer load. CODEOWNERS-based automatic assignment is the first lever — it routes each PR to the smallest set of reviewers with relevant domain knowledge, rather than broadcasting to the whole team. Keeping PRs small (under 400 changed lines) is the second — small PRs complete review 3-5x faster than large ones and have measurably lower defect escape rates.

Automated pre-review tooling — static analysis, secret scanning, automated eval gates — shifts a class of findings from human review time to CI time. A blocker that CI catches in two minutes is less expensive than a reviewer spending twenty minutes finding the same issue. Every false-positive the human reviewer does not have to examine is reviewer capacity returned to the pool.

For very large teams (50+ engineers in the same agent monorepo), consider a 'draft review' step: a lightweight async pass where a senior engineer flags whether a PR needs deep security review before full review is scheduled. This prioritizes the review queue and prevents security-team bottlenecks from stalling low-risk PRs.
Tooling & ecosystem
Review platforms
GitHub Pull Requests
— industry-standard; CODEOWNERS, branch protection, and required status checks are native features. GitHub Actions provides the CI layer.
GitLab Merge Requests
— equivalent feature set; CODEOWNERS called CODEOWNERS or .gitlab/CODEOWNERS; CI via .gitlab-ci.yml.
Gerrit
— used in large monorepos (Google, Android); stronger change tracking than GitHub but steeper tooling overhead.
Automated analysis tools
Ruff
— fast Python linter and formatter; configure as a required CI check so reviewers never spend time on style.
Semgrep
— semantic code analysis with rules for Python, JavaScript, and custom patterns; ships with rulesets for common security anti-patterns in LLM-connected code.
Bandit
— Python-specific security linter; catches hardcoded secrets, use of unsafe functions, and insecure defaults.
Gitleaks / TruffleHog
— scan diff for secret patterns; run in CI before any human reviewer touches the PR.
Review assistance

# Install and run Semgrep with the LLM-security ruleset on the PR diff
pip install semgrep
git diff main...HEAD --name-only | xargs semgrep --config "p/owasp-top-ten" --config "p/python"

# Run Bandit on changed Python files only
git diff main...HEAD --name-only --diff-filter=AM | grep '\.py$' | xargs bandit -ll

# Run Gitleaks on the full repo history since branch point
gitleaks detect --source . --log-opts "main..HEAD" --verbose
GitHub CLI for review automation

# List open PRs awaiting your review
gh pr list --search "review-requested:@me" --json number,title,author,createdAt

# View a PR diff in the terminal before leaving the editor
gh pr diff 142

# Approve a PR after review
gh pr review 142 --approve --body "LGTM — eval results look good, no security concerns."

# Request changes with a comment
gh pr review 142 --request-changes --body "Please add an allowlist check for CC addresses."

# Check CI status before approving
gh pr checks 142
Comparison with alternatives
Code review is one mechanism in a broader quality and safety toolkit. The table below compares it to the most commonly used complementary or alternative approaches.
Approach
What it catches
What it misses
Best used for
Peer code review
Semantic bugs, design issues, prompt/tool risks, missing tests
Issues the reviewer lacks expertise to see
All production merges in team repos
Automated linting / static analysis
Style, type errors, obvious code smells, secret patterns
Semantic logic errors, behavioral risks
CI gate before human review
Automated eval suite (CI)
Behavioral regression against golden dataset
Risks outside golden set coverage
Prompt and tool changes
Pair programming
Real-time design feedback, fewer review iterations
Asynchronous teams; no written record
Complex, high-risk features early in design
Architecture review board
System-level design risks before implementation
Implementation details, line-level bugs
New agent capabilities or major refactors
Formal verification
Mathematical correctness of critical algorithms
Most agent code (prompts, I/O, side effects)
Safety-critical deterministic logic only
Security & reliability
Threat: prompt injection via untrusted content

An agent that processes external content — emails, web pages, user messages — is exposed to prompt injection: an attacker embeds instructions in content the agent reads, attempting to hijack its behavior. Code review is the point where this threat must be identified. Reviewers should ask: does this code path allow user-controlled content to reach the agent's system prompt or tool-invocation logic without sanitisation?
Threat: excessive tool permissions

Agents with broad tool permissions are dangerous: a single confused-deputy error or prompt injection can trigger a high-impact action. Reviewers should enforce the principle of least privilege on every new tool — if the tool needs read access to a bucket, it should not receive write access 'for convenience'. Reviewer questions to ask: what is the minimum permission this tool needs? What is the worst case if the tool is called with attacker-controlled arguments?
Threat: secret leakage in diffs

Developers sometimes accidentally commit secrets in configuration files or test fixtures. A secret scanner (Gitleaks, TruffleHog) in CI catches this before the PR is reviewed, but reviewers should also watch for secrets in log statements, error messages, and environment variable defaults that are printed at startup.
Reliability: reviewer availability

A review gate that requires a specific individual creates a reliability risk: if that person is on leave, the deployment pipeline stops. Design team ownership, not individual ownership: CODEOWNERS should point to teams of at least two people per path, and each team should maintain a rotation of active reviewers. Document the escalation path for urgent reviews.
Reliability: stale approvals

An approval given before the author addresses requested changes is meaningless if new commits are not reviewed. Enable 'dismiss stale reviews on push' in branch protection settings so that every new commit requires fresh approval. This is especially critical for agent repos where a prompt change could be smuggled into a PR after security approval.
Mental model

Think of code review as a pre-flight checklist before an autonomous agent takes off. Automated CI checks the instruments — fuel (tests pass), altimeter (lint clean), engine pressure (evals within threshold). Human review verifies the flight plan: is the destination the right one? Is the route approved? Does the crew have authority to fly this corridor? Neither replaces the other. A plane that passes instrument checks but has an unapproved route is still unsafe to fly. A crew that reviews the plan but ignores the instrument failure lights takes off anyway. Both gates must pass.
Key takeaways
Code review is the last human gate before autonomous agent behavior reaches production — treat it with the weight that implies.
In agent repos, review scope extends beyond code to prompts, tool schemas, permission manifests, and eval results — all of these can change agent behavior without touching a single Python function.
Use CODEOWNERS to route prompt changes to the ML team, tool changes to platform and security, and infrastructure changes to devops — right expertise on every PR automatically.
Keep PRs small and focused. Review effectiveness drops sharply above 400 changed lines; a mega-PR covering prompts, tools, and infra together is effectively unreviewed.
Automate everything automatable — linting, secret scanning, eval gate — so human reviewers can spend their attention on semantic and behavioral risk rather than style.
Require two approvals for any change that grants a destructive capability or weakens a safety constraint; one reviewer catching a risk is better than none, but two is a meaningful redundancy check.
Enforce 'dismiss stale reviews on push' so that a security approval cannot be laundered through a subsequent commit that changes the prompt or tool scope.
Review SLA matters: a PR that waits days for review creates pressure to approve without reading. Define and measure a target first-response time and treat violations as a team capacity signal.
